coredata.dc:title: Low toxicity ginsenoside Rg1-carbon nanodots as a potential therapeutic agent for human non-small cell lung cancer

coredata.dc:description: Here ginsenoside Rg1 was used to synthesise Rg1 carbon nanodots via a one-step hydrothermal method. The surface of the Rg1 carbon nanodots is rich in hydrophilic functional groups with good water solubility and biocompatibility. The Rg1 carbon nanodots exhibited a high inhibitory effect on the proliferation, migration, and proapoptotic ability of non-small cell lung cancer A549 cells. The changes in the levels of ROS, Ca2+, and MMP in A549 cells after the administration of Rg1 carbon nanodots were evaluated and further correlated with relevant proteins in the caspase apoptotic pathway. Proteomic screening revealed that the Rg1 carbon nanodots could regulate A549 cell apoptosis by activating the expression of MAPK pathway-related proteins. In the in vivo experiment, the therapeutic efficacy of the Rg1 carbon nanodots in inhibiting tumour growth was much higher than that of commonly used chemotherapy drugs, with negligible toxicity and side effects. Immunohistochemical staining showed that the expression of caspase- and MAPK pathway-related proteins in mouse tumour tissues was consistent with that at the cellular level. The results suggest that Rg1 carbon nanodots can promote tumour apoptosis and represent a potential therapeutic agent for human non-small-cell lung cancer.

The insets show photographs of Rg1-CDs under sunlight (left) and 365 nm UV light (right). (d) Fluorescence spectra of Rg1-CDs under different excitation wavelengths (350–480 nm). (e) Fluorescence attenuation curve of the Rg1-CDs solution. (f) Fourier transform infrared spectra of Rg1 and Rg1-CDs. (g) XPS spectra of Rg1-CDs. High-resolution XPS spectra of Rg1-CDs of C 1 s (h) C 1 s and (i) O 1 s.

(a) Cytotoxicity profile of A549 cells treated with Rg1-CDs and (b) Rg1 at different concentrations after 48 h. (c) Cytotoxicity profile of HEK 293 T and (d) LO2 cells treated with different concentrations of Rg1-CDs after 48 h. (e) Cellular morphology of treated A549 cells; scale bars: 200 μm. (f) Bright-field and fluorescence microscope images of A549 cells incubated with Rg1-CDs at different times; scale bars: 400 μm. The data are presented as the mean ± standard deviation (n = 6), * P < 0.05, ** P < 0.01, and *** P < 0.001 compared to 0 μg/mL.

(c) Corresponding statistical histogram of (a). (d) Corresponding statistical histogram of (b). Scale bars: 400 μm. * P < 0.05, ** P < 0.01, and *** P < 0.001 compared to 0 μg/mL.

(c) Computed tomography scan and tumour photos of mice. (d) Statistic chart of tumour weight of mice on the 21st day. (e) Calculated chart of weight growth rate of mice. (f) H&E staining, TUNEL staining, and IHC images of mouse tumours; scale bars: 400 μm. * P < 0.05, ** P < 0.01, and *** P < 0.001 compared to Model group. ^ P < 0.05, ^^ P < 0.01, and ^^^ P < 0.001 compared to the CDDP group.

head.ce:title: Low toxicity ginsenoside Rg1-carbon nanodots as a potential therapeutic agent for human non-small cell lung cancer

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Here ginsenoside Rg1 was used to synthesise Rg1 carbon nanodots via a one-step hydrothermal method. The surface of the Rg1 carbon nanodots is rich in hydrophilic functional groups with good water solubility and biocompatibility. The Rg1 carbon nanodots exhibited a high inhibitory effect on the proliferation, migration, and proapoptotic ability of non-small cell lung cancer A549 cells. The changes in the levels of ROS, Ca2+, and MMP in A549 cells after the administration of Rg1 carbon nanodots were evaluated and further correlated with relevant proteins in the caspase apoptotic pathway. Proteomic screening revealed that the Rg1 carbon nanodots could regulate A549 cell apoptosis by activating the expression of MAPK pathway-related proteins. In the in vivo experiment, the therapeutic efficacy of the Rg1 carbon nanodots in inhibiting tumour growth was much higher than that of commonly used chemotherapy drugs, with negligible toxicity and side effects. Immunohistochemical staining showed that the expression of caspase- and MAPK pathway-related proteins in mouse tumour tissues was consistent with that at the cellular level. The results suggest that Rg1 carbon nanodots can promote tumour apoptosis and represent a potential therapeutic agent for human non-small-cell lung cancer.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Non-small-cell lung cancer (NSCLC) is the most common type of lung cancer, accounting for more than 85 % of lung cancer cases [1–3]. Due to the specificity of tumours, the effectiveness of targeted therapy and immunotherapy varies greatly among different patients. Only a portion of patients are suitable candidates for these treatments, whereas the majority rely on popular chemotherapeutic agents (e.g., platinum-based drugs, antimetabolites, anthracyclines) [4]. Unfortunately, these chemical drugs are accompanied by strong toxic effects. After treatment, the overall survival rate of patients with NSCLC remains low, with only 19 % surviving for more than 5 years [5,6]. Therefore, to improve treatment efficacy, survival rate, and quality of life, it is important to develop anticancer drugs with good treatment effects and low toxicity and side effects.

As an emerging nanomaterial, carbon dots (CDs) have various preparation sources, simple preparation processes, and unique optical properties. Because of their abundant surface functional groups and negligible long-term biological toxicity, CDs have been widely used in the biomedical field as bioprobes, biosensors, and drug delivery carriers. More significantly, several recent studies have shown that CDs prepared with drug-active precursors can retain part of the structure and drug activity of the precursors. By virtue of their unique size and surface properties, CDs can be engulfed by cells in large quantities through endocytosis, which can effectively enhance or even produce novel drug effects [7,8]. For example, our group prepared cefminox sodium carbon nanodots (CS-CDs) with cefminox sodium as the precursor. CS-CDs retained part of the structure of cefminox sodium, with lower biotoxicity and stronger antimicrobial activity. CS-CDs can effectively treat bloodstream infections and can also quickly and quantitatively detect the proportion of Escherichia coli in the blood [9]. Yao et al. [10] synthesised chlorogenic acid carbon quantum dots (ChA CQDs) from the biologically active chlorogenic acid present in coffee. ChA CQDs induced ferroptosis in HepG2 cells by promoting glutathione depletion and glutathione peroxidase 4 inactivation. Xu et al. [11] prepared biological CDs derived from red dates and found that they could be used to effectively treat cancer-related anaemia. Lu et al. [12] obtained semi-carbonised nanodots (SCNDs) from burnt medicinal herbal atractylodes. SCNDs exhibited a strong anti-stress gastric ulcer effect with an inhibition rate of up to 90 %. SCNDs also showed extremely high biocompatibility and low toxicity. Liang et al. [13] prepared low-toxicity glycyrrhizic acid (Gly-CDs) using glycyrrhizic acid, a herbal extract, as a carbon source. Gly-CDs had higher biological activity than glycyrrhizic acid molecules and common CDs and have been shown to be useful for inhibiting a variety of viruses.

The advantages of using herbal medicines for disease treatment are that most natural plant extracts have low toxicity, minimal side effects, low cost, and high patient acceptance [14,15]. Yet, many herbal extracts have limited efficacy and value in clinical applications due to poor water solubility and biofilm permeability [16,17]. Among numerous natural herbal medicines, ginsenoside are a class of plant extracts with various pharmacological activities [16,18,19]. A previous study by our group [20] revealed that carbon nanodots prepared from ginseng total saponin (GS-CDs) had a specific inhibitory effect against human neuroblastoma. Because the precursor ginseng total saponin is derived from natural plant extracts, GS-CDs have low biological toxicity and side effects and are expected to be used as a potential therapeutic drug against infantile neuroblastoma in the future. On this basis, we propose to select ginsenoside monomer compounds as precursors for the synthesis of carbon dots and explore their potential applications as an anti-tumour therapy.

Rg1 belongs to the class of protopanaxatriol saponins. Studies have found that Rg1 has certain pharmacodynamic effects in the protection of various organs and the treatment of various diseases, including anti-tumour activity. However, the medicinal effects of Rg1 are limited because of the difficulty of transshipment across cell membranes and the low cell absorption rate in the molecular state of Rg1 [13,21–23]. In this study, ginsenoside Rg1 was used as the sole carbon source, and a low-cost and green hydrothermal method was used to synthesise fluorescent Rg1 carbon nanodots (Rg1-CDs) in one step. No other chemical reagents were added during the synthesis process, thereby avoiding the potential for unintended biological toxicity. The Rg1-CDs were stable with a diameter of approximately 4.0 ± 1.4 nm, and good water solubility and biocompatibility. In vitro experiments showed that Rg1-CDs could penetrate the cell membrane, be taken up by human NSCLC cells (A549) in large amounts, and exert an effective inhibitory effect against A549 cells. The results of the in vivo experiments demonstrated that Rg1-CDs highly inhibited NSCLC. Mechanistic studies confirmed that Rg1-CDs inhibited proliferation and promoted A549 cell apoptosis by regulating the expression of caspase- and MAPK pathway-involved proteins. With its low cost, green preparation process, and low biological toxicity, Rg1-CDs are expected to become a new chemotherapy drug for the treatment of NSCLC in the future, with high clinical and social application value ( Scheme 1).

The prepared Rg1-CDs were uniform in size with a diameter of 4.0 ± 1.4 nm and were well dispersed ( Fig. 1a, b). The Rg1 aqueous solution was transparent and exhibited little fluorescence under UV light at 365 nm (Fig. S1). Due to the absence of a conjugated structure, the Rg1 solution displayed negligible UV absorption. The prepared Rg1-CDs aqueous solution was yellow under sunlight and emitted blue-green fluorescence under UV light at 365 nm (Fig. 1c). The Rg1-CDs exhibited UV absorption bands at ∼225 nm and ∼283 nm, which were attributed to the π-π* and n-π* transitions, respectively. The Rg1-CDs exhibited fluorescence excitation-dependent properties (Fig. 1d). At an optimal excitation wavelength of 360 nm, the emission wavelength of the Rg1-CDs was ∼440 nm. The fluorescence decay curve of the Rg1-CDs was fitted with a third-order attenuation exponential function (Fig. 1d). Three fluorescence lifetimes (τ) were obtained, indicating that the Rg1-CDs have three fluorescence centres (Table S1). The average fluorescence lifetime of the Rg1-CDs was 4.44 ns, and the quantum yield was 2.1 %. Due to the few conjugated fluorophores in the Rg1 molecule, the appearance of UV absorption bands and fluorescence of the Rg1-CDs can be attributed to the cross-linked enhanced emission (CEE) effect, because of their special nano structure [24]. This immobilization enhancement mode is widely present in various luminescent materials, such as the well-known aggregation-induced emission phenomenon in organic molecules and the amorphous carbon polymer dots [25–27]. Rg1 molecules typically contain a limited number of conjugated fluorophore groups such as carbon-carbon double bonds, carbon-carbon triple bonds, and carbon-based π electrons. The main sources of UV absorption and fluorescence emission of the prepared Rg1-CDs did not consist of these classical conjugated structures but were generated by non-classical conjugation during the hydrothermal reaction, resulting in low quantum yields [20,24]. The FT-IR spectra showed that the Rg1-CDs had absorption peaks at 3377, 2927, 2887, 1664, 1519, 1396, 1069, and 1026 cm−1, which were attributed to the vibration and rotation of O-H, C-H, CO, and C-O functional groups (Fig. 1f). These results support that the Rg1-CDs retain part of the structure and functional groups of Rg1 [13,20]. In the XPS spectra of the Rg1-CDs, the electron binding energy positions of element C were 284.8, 286.3, and 287.5 eV, corresponding to the C-C/CC, C-O, and CO bonds, respectively (Fig. 1g, h). The electron-binding energy positions of element O were 531.4 and 532.8 eV, corresponding to the C-O bond and CO bond, respectively (Fig. 1i). Both the IR and XPS analyses showed that the Rg1-CDs were rich in -OH and -COOH, and hence should display good hydrophilicity and biocompatibility.

Stability is an important indicator of whether the Rg1-CDs can be used as nanomedicines. The stability of the Rg1-CDs under different conditions was investigated by detecting changes in their fluorescence emission and UV–vis absorption properties. Across a large concentration range of NaCl and KCl (0–1.0 mol/L), the UV–vis absorption peak position and fluorescence intensity of the Rg1-CDs did not change significantly (Figs. S2, S3). This implies that the Rg1-CDs have good salt resistance (the normal salt concentration in human body fluids is ∼0.15 mol/L). Considering the different pH values of various parts of organisms, the fluorescence emission and UV–vis absorption properties of the Rg1-CDs was also studied at different pH values (2−10) (Figs. S4, S5). Little change was observed in the position of the fluorescent peak and the UV–vis absorption of the Rg1-CDs, indicating that their good pH stability. Under relatively extreme pH conditions (pH=2 and pH=10), the UV absorption intensity and fluorescence intensity of the Rg1-CDs exhibited some fluctuations, likely due to the change in the pH of the external environment, which affected the surface properties of the Rg1-CDs containing carboxyl and hydroxyl groups, resulting in changes in the fluorescence intensity of the system. The Rg1-CDs were stored at 4°C for 14 days, and their fluorescence properties remained stable (Fig. S6). Under UV irradiation for 7 h, the fluorescence properties of the Rg1-CDs were also stable (Fig. S7). This indicates that the Rg1-CDs have good anti-optical interference performance and can be used as a fluorescent probe for imaging and tracking while being used in nanomedicine.

The results of the Cell Counting Kit-8 assay (CCK-8) illustrated that the proliferation of A549 cells was significantly inhibited after culturing with different concentrations of the Rg1-CDs for 48 h. The inhibition rate gradually increased with increasing concentration, in an obviously dose-dependent manner. When the administration concentration of the Rg1-CDs reached 90 μg/mL, the inhibition rate of A549 cells reached ∼67 % (P < 0.001) ( Fig. 2a). In contrast, Rg1 at the same concentration slightly inhibited A549 cells (P > 0.05) (Fig. 2b). It is presumed that Rg1 in its molecular state cannot be effectively taken up by cells via cell transport and exert its medicinal effects. Due to their nanoscale properties and nanostructure, the Rg1-CDs can much more effectively penetrate the cell membrane and be taken up by tumour cells, leading to superior medicinal effects against A549 cells [28–30]. Since the precursor of the Rg1-CDs was a non-toxic herbal extract, the Rg1-CDs showed little toxicity to human renal epithelial cells (HEK 293 T) or human normal hepatocytes (LO2) at the same concentrations (P > 0.05) (Fig. 2c, d). The corresponding IC50 calculation results also indicated that the Rg1-CDs had lower cytotoxicity (Table S2) [31–33].

The effects of different concentrations of the Rg1-CDs on the morphology of A549 cells were observed using microscopy after coculturing for 48 h. The cells in the 0 μg/mL Rg1-CDs group were uniform in size, with clear nuclei and large volumes, perfectly round shapes, and complete cell membrane coatings. With an increase in the concentration of the Rg1-CDs (50, 70, and 90 μg/mL), the cell size became uneven, the cell volume decreased, the cell morphology became irregular, and the cell membrane shrank. Additionally, the number of cells decreased significantly, with many cell fragments observed in the field of view (Fig. 2e).

At the same time, the entry of the Rg1-CDs (90 μg/mL) into A549 cells was monitored using fluorescence microscopy (Fig. 2f). In the fluorescence images, the Rg1-CDs were taken up through the cell membrane and mainly enriched the cytoplasm of A549 cells. The fluorescence intensity of the cells gradually increased with increasing incubation time, indicating that the number of internalized the Rg1-CDs increased with time. When co-incubated for 12 h, the intracellular fluorescence intensity was the most intense, indicating that the number of the Rg1-CDs taken up by the cells reached the highest level. A small number of cells had become round and shrunken in the brightfield, and the cell structure was destroyed. This suggests that the Rg1-CDs interacted with the intracellular matrix during this period. Co-incubation was continued until 48 h, and the fluorescence intensity within the cells gradually decreased. It is speculated that some of the Rg1-CDs may be metabolised and excreted from cells via exocytosis. A large number of the Rg1-CDs may have reacted with the intracellular matrix, leading to their nanostructure being decomposed and destroyed, resulting in a decrease in the fluorescence intensity of the system, accompanied by a large number of cells shrinking and dying (observed under brightfield).

A clone formation assay was used to determine the proliferation of A549 cells after 48 h of co-incubation with different concentrations of the Rg1-CDs. It was found that the cell communities were smaller and less abundant after co-incubation with different concentrations of the Rg1-CDs (30, 50, 70, and 90 μg/mL) compared to the 0 μg/mL Rg1-CDs group. This result demonstrates that the proliferation of A549 cells was significantly inhibited by the Rg1-CDs exposure in a concentration-dependent manner (Fig. S8).

A scratch assay was used to observe the healing ability of A549 cells after treatment with different concentrations of the Rg1-CDs ( Fig. 3a, c). Compared to the 0 μg/mL Rg1-CDs group, the healing capacity of A549 cells in the Rg1-CDs groups (50, 70, and 90 μg/mL) decreased clearly (P < 0.01). After treatment for 48 h, the corresponding healing rates were 90, 64, 60, 56, and 41 %. Compared to the 0 μg/mL Rg1-CDs group, the healing capacity of A549 cells in the Rg1-CDs groups (30, 50, 70, and 90 μg/mL) was markedly reduced (P < 0.05, P < 0.01, P < 0.001), indicating that the Rg1-CDs effectively inhibited the healing capacity of A549 cells. The Transwell assay was used to evaluate the effect of different concentrations of the Rg1-CDs on the migration of A549 cells (Fig. 3b, d). After 24 and 48 h, the amount of A549 cell migration in the Rg1-CDs groups (70 and 90 μg/mL) was notably reduced compared to the 0 μg/mL Rg1-CDs group (P < 0.01), demonstrating that the Rg1-CDs effectively inhibit the migration of A549 cells.

Flow cytometry was used to detect the effects of different concentrations of the Rg1-CDs on the cell cycle and apoptosis of A549 cells. After 48 h of treatment with the Rg1-CDs, the G1/G0, S, and G2/M phases of A549 cells in the Rg1-CDs groups (30, 50, and 70 μg/mL) changed slightly compared to that in the 0 μg/mL Rg1-CDs group (P > 0.05). When the concentration of the Rg1-CDs reached 90 μg/mL, the proportion of A549 cells in G2/M statistically increased (P < 0.01), and the proportion of A549 cells in G1/G0 phase statistically decreased (P < 0.001) ( Fig. 4a). The results indicate that at a concentration of 90 μg/mL, Rg1-CDs induced significant G2/M phase arrest in A549 cells.

A549 cells were treated with different concentrations of the Rg1-CDs for 48 h, before detecting the rates of apoptosis. At concentrations of 30 and 50 μg/mL, the Rg1-CDs tended to induce apoptosis in A549 cells. When the concentration of the Rg1-CDs was 30 μg/mL, the early and late apoptosis rates of A549 cells were 3.23 and 7.45 %, respectively (P > 0.05). At a concentration of 50 μg/mL of the Rg1-CDs, the early and late apoptosis rates of A549 cells were 5.58 and 7.87 %, respectively (P > 0.05). When the concentration of the Rg1-CDs was 70 μg/mL, the early apoptosis rate of A549 cells was 13.73 %, while the late apoptosis rate increased significantly to 12.25 % (P < 0.05). When the concentration of the Rg1-CDs was 90 μg/mL, both the early and late apoptosis rates of A549 cells significantly increased, reaching 24.03 and 20.40 %, respectively (P < 0.01, P < 0.001) (Fig. 4b). This result demonstrates that the Rg1-CDs can significantly induce apoptosis in A549 cells in a concentration-dependent manner.

In general, changes in the levels of reactive oxygen species (ROS), Ca2+, and mitochondrial membrane potential (MMP) play important roles in apoptosis [34–39]. We detected synergistic changes in ROS, Ca2+, and MMP levels and correlated them with related apoptotic proteins in the caspase pathway to explore the role of the Rg1-CDs in promoting apoptosis in A549 cells.

Accordingly, changes in the fluorescence intensities of the indicators of ROS, Ca2+, and MMP in A549 cells after 48 h of treatment with different concentrations of the Rg1-CDs were observed via fluorescence microscopy ( Fig. 5a, b). The A549 cells were morphologically intact in the 0 μg/mL Rg1-CDs group (Fig. S9). The fluorescence of ROS and Ca2+ was faint, whereas that of MMP was strong. With increasing Rg1-CDs concentration, the red fluorescence of labeled ROS and the green fluorescence of labeled Ca2+ in the cells gradually increased, and the red fluorescence of MMP gradually decreased. At a concentration of 50 μg/mL of the Rg1-CDs, the average fluorescence of labelled ROS in A549 cells was enhanced (P < 0.05), while that of labelled Ca2+ and MMP was not significant (P > 0.05). When the concentration of the Rg1-CDs was increased to 70 μg/mL and 90 μg/mL, the red fluorescence of labelled ROS and the green fluorescence of labelled Ca2+ were significantly enhanced (P < 0.01 and P < 0.001), while the average fluorescence of labelled MMP decreased significantly (P > 0.05 and P < 0.05). At the Rg1-CDs concentration of 90 μg/mL, the statistical average fluorescence level of the label MMP dropped by 48.8 % inside the A549 cells. This result demonstrates that the intracellular ROS levels were elevated and Ca2+ accumulation was significant, resulting in mitochondrial membrane damage and decreased MMP levels [37]. Correspondingly, the structure of A549 cells was damaged, and the number of apoptotic cells increased (Fig. S9).

We next sought to investigate the association with apoptosis induced by changes in intracellular ROS levels, Ca2+ concentrations, and MMP levels, and to conduct an overall evaluation of whether the Rg1-CDs induced apoptosis in A549 cells through the caspase apoptosis pathway. Briefly, the Rg1-CDs (90 μg/mL) were incubated with A549 cells for 48 h, and the expression levels of the anti-apoptotic protein Bcl-2 and the pro-apoptotic proteins Bax, Cytochrome-c (Cyto-c), Caspase-9 (Cas-9) and Caspase-3 (Cas-3) were detected by western blotting (Fig. 5c). Compared to the Model group, the expression of Bcl-2 in A549 cells was downregulated (P < 0.05) and the expression of Bax, Cyto-c, Cas-9, and Cas-3 were upregulated (P < 0.05) after the Rg1-CDs treatment. We investigated changes in the ROS, Ca2+, and MMP levels simultaneously and further evaluated their relationships with apoptotic proteins in the caspase pathway. The results confirmed that the Rg1-CDs treatment with A549 cells for 48 h led to an increase in ROS and Ca2+ levels, resulting in rupture of the mitochondrial outer membrane and a decrease in MMP levels. Meanwhile, the inversion of the expression levels of Bcl-2 and Bax induced by the Rg1-CDs treatment also led to the formation of mitochondrial outer membrane pores and a further decrease in MMP levels [40]. Cyto-c was then released from the fragmented outer mitochondrial membrane and outer mitochondrial membrane pores, activating the apoptosis-related proteins Cas-9 and Cas-3 in the caspase pathway and inducing apoptosis in A549 cells [41].

Proteomics analysis was used to compare the changes in protein content and distribution in A549 cells before and after the Rg1-CDs treatment (90 μg/mL) to further study the mechanism by which the Rg1-CDs inhibit proliferation and promote apoptosis in A549 cells ( Fig. 6). A total of 6668 proteins were identified by tandem mass tag (TMT) mass spectrometry for label-free quantification of differentially expressed proteins in A549 cells in the Model group (before the Rg1-CDs treatment) and the Rg1-CDs group (after the Rg1-CDs treatment). Bioinformatics analysis was used to screen differentially expressed proteins (upregulated or downregulated, P < 0.05). Between the Model and the Rg1-CDs group, 338 differentially expressed proteins were screened, of which 199 were upregulated and 139 were downregulated (Fig. 6a, see Table S3 for details). The volcano and differential protein cluster analysis plots show the regulation of differential proteins (Fig. 6b, c).

The functions of differential proteins were described using biological processes (BP), molecular function (MF), and cellular components (CC) in the Gene Ontology (GO) database (Fig. 6d). MF analysis showed that the differential proteins were mainly involved in catalytic activity, molecular function regulation, and structural molecule activity. The Rg1-CDs played a significant role in BP, including cellular processes, metabolic processes, and biological regulation. CC analysis showed that the differential proteins were mainly present in cells, cell parts, and organelles.

The Kyoto Encyclopedia of Genes and Genomes (KEGG) database was used to analyse the KEGG pathway for differentially expressed proteins. The results showed that the differential proteins were mainly involved in the MAPK, PI3K-Akt, and lipid and atherosclerosis pathways (Fig. 6e). Among them, the MAPK pathway is closely related to apoptosis and can be activated by various stimuli, including ROS, cytokines, and UV light [42,43]. Activation of the MAPK signalling pathway induces tumour cell death, which is the mechanism by which various anticancer drugs induce cancer cell death [44,45]. After treatment with the Rg1-CDs for 48 h, related proteins in the MAPK pathway in A549 cells were selected for western blot verification ( Fig. 7). The results illustrated that the Rg1-CDs upregulated the expression of p-MKK3, MKK3, p-p38, p38, p-HSP27, and HSP27 in the MAPK signalling pathway (P < 0.001), and downregulated the expression of MSK1 (P < 0.001). MKK3 and p38 are important kinases of the MAPK family, located upstream of the MAPK signalling pathway and involved in cell differentiation, division, and death. MKK3 has a dual regulatory effect on cancer cell growth [46,47]. Activated MKK3 can specifically phosphorylate p38, which, together with phosphorylated p38 is a key regulator of autophagy and apoptosis. The upregulation of these proteins can lead to cell death. Moreover, p38 is an upstream protein of HSP27 and MSK1 and is responsible for controlling their activation. MSK1 and HSP27 play important roles in the regulation of cell proliferation, cytoskeletal remodelling, and apoptosis [48–50]. MSK1 can directly activate and phosphorylate HSP27. The phosphorylation of HSP27 further contributes to p38-induced apoptosis [51,52]. The western blot results confirmed that the Rg1-CDs regulated the expression of related proteins in the MAPK pathway (Fig. 7a). The Rg1-CDs upregulated MKK3 expression in A549 cells to specifically phosphorylate p38. Phosphorylated p38 interacted with MSK1 to prompt its ubiquitination and degradation in the cytoplasm. Then, p38 and MSK1 promoted HSP27 phosphorylation and finally induced apoptosis in A549 cells. These results suggest that the Rg1-CDs effectively induce apoptosis in A549 cells by influencing related proteins in the MAPK pathway (Fig. 7b). Moreover, cell cycle arrest and cell apoptosis are closely related [53,54]. Chen et al. found that ROS production was able to activate phosphorylation of p38, further influencing the expression of downstream proteins p53 and p21, making mice ovarian granulosa cell arrest at G2/M phase [55]. It is similar to the results of cell cycle (Fig. 4a) and proteomics analysis in A549 cells after Rg1-CDs administration.

The NSCLC tumour-bearing mouse model was established by subcutaneously injecting A549 cells into BALB/c nude mice. An inhibitory effect of the Rg1-CDs on the tumour growth of the mice was observed. The nude mice used in the experiment were randomly divided into five groups (n = 5): tumour-bearing mice were injected, via the tail vein, with normal saline (Model group), Rg1-CDs (Rg1-CDs group), or cisplatin (CDDP group); normal mice were injected, via the tail vein, with normal saline (Control group) or Rg1-CDs (Control+Rg1-CDs group). Under the same conditions, the experimental monitoring period was set to 21 days ( Fig. 8). The tumour volume of the mice was measured in real time every 2 days (Fig. 8a). Initially, the tumour growth rate of mice in each group was relatively slow. From day 11 onwards, the tumour growth rate of mice in the Model group increased rapidly, and the tumour volume on day 21 was ∼12 times that of the initial tumour volume. The tumour growth of the mice in the CDDP group was relatively slow, with a tumour inhibition rate on day 21 of 48 %. The tumour growth rate of mice in the Rg1-CDs group was the slowest, with a tumour inhibition rate on day 21 of 83 % (Fig. 8b). On day 21, the tumour size of the mice was carefully detected using computed tomography. Compared to the tumour volume in the Model group, the CDDP group had a smaller tumour volume, and the Rg1-CDs group had the smallest tumour volume (Fig. 8c). These results were consistent with the photos of tumours removed after sacrifice on that day. The removed tumours were weighed at the same time. Compared to the Model group, the tumour weight in the CDDP group decreased (P < 0.05), and the tumour weight in the Rg1-CDs group was significantly reduced (P < 0.001). The tumour weight of mice was significantly lower in the Rg1-CDs group than in the CDDP group (P < 0.001) (Fig. 8d). These results indicate that Rg1-CDs have a good inhibitory effect on NSCLC tumours in mice, and that the inhibitory effect is superior to that of the clinic control drug CDDP. This may be due to the susceptibility of A549 cells to develop resistance to cisplatin [56].

In addition, interleukin abnormalities in the body are closely related to the growth and development of cancer [57,58]. For example, the pro-inflammatory factor interleukin 6 (IL-6) and the anti-inflammatory factor interleukin 10 (IL-10) are usually involved in tumour invasion and metastasis progression in vivo [59]. The levels of IL-6 and IL-10 in the serum of mice in each group were measured using enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) and discussed (Fig. S10). These findings suggest that Rg1-CDs reduce inflammation in the blood of mice and inhibit tumour malignancy and metastasis to some extent.

During the entire experiment, the changes in the state and body weight of mice in the Control group, Control+Rg1-CDs group, Model group, CDDP group and Rg1-CDs group were monitored in real time (Fig. 8e). The mice in the Control group, Control+Rg1-CDs group, and Rg1-CDs group were always in good condition, with gradually increasing body weight. This indicates that Rg1-CDs have minimal toxicity and side effects during long-term lung cancer treatment. The increase in body weight of mice in the Model group was relatively slow, possibly due to the rapid growth of tumours. The weight loss of mice was clear in the CDDP group and increased with increasing injection volume of CDDP. After CDDP injection, the mice were listless and anorexic, and the amount of food consumed was significantly reduced due to the toxic side effects of CDDP. Photographs of the staining of the major organs of mice in the five groups were compared. H&E staining of major organs of mice in the Control+Rg1-CDs group, Model group and Rg1-CDs group was similar to that of mice in the Control group, with few histopathological changes (Fig. S11). The liver cells of mice in the CDDP group were disordered and swollen, with mild local vacuolar degeneration. The arrangement of renal tissue was also disordered, with nuclear pyknosis, partial dilation of renal tubules, and enlargement of the lumens. This result implies that CDDP treatment can cause a certain degree of liver and kidney damage in mice, while the Rg1-CDs show good biosafety.

H&E and TUNEL staining were used to evaluate the pathological damage and apoptosis of NSCLC tumours in mice after treatment with the Rg1-CDs (Fig. 8f). The tumour cells of mice in the Model group were tightly arranged, with few apoptotic cells. Apoptotic cells appeared in the tumour tissue of mice in the CDDP group. The tumour cells of mice in the Rg1-CDs group were loosely arranged, and the number of apoptotic cells was highest. As neovascularization is necessary for tumour growth, an endothelial cell adhesion molecule (CD31) was selected to label endothelial cells of the tumour vascular [60]. Cyclin-dependent kinase 4 (CDK4) and nuclear-associated antigen (Ki67) are pathologic molecular biomarkers of lung cancer and are highly expressed in lung cancer tissues [61]. Neuron-specific enolase (NSE) is a critical marker for the clinical diagnosis of tumours [62]. Accordingly, immunohistochemical (IHC) staining was used to observe the expression of these markers in mouse tumour tissues after the Rg1-CDs treatment. The results showed that CD31, CDK4, Ki67, and NSE were highly expressed in the tumour tissues of mice in the Model group, showed less expression in the CDDP group, and the lowest expression in the Rg1-CDs group. This indicates that CDDP and Rg1-CDs can induce apoptosis in NSCLC tumours of mice, and the pro-apoptotic effect of the Rg1-CDs is superior to that of CDDP.

To verify the mechanistic in vitro western blot results at the cellular level shown in Figs. 5c and 7a, the changes in the expression levels of caspase and MAPK pathway-related proteins were compared by IHC staining of mouse NSCLC tumour tissues in the Model group and the Rg1-CDs group. (Figs. S12–S18). The results demonstrated the effect of the Rg1-CDs administration on the expression of Bcl-2, Bax, Cyto-c, Cas-9, Cas-3, MKK3, p-MKK3, p38, p-p38, MSK1, and HSP27 proteins in NSCLC tumour tissues. Once again, the Rg1-CDs were shown to inhibit NSCLC tumour growth by regulating both the caspase and MAPK pathways in vitro and in vivo.

Finally, we assessed acute the Rg1-CDs toxicity in normal ICR mice (n = 5) (Fig. S19). The results clearly showed that high-dose Rg1-CDs treatment had little effect on the health of mice. The Rg1-CDs show good biocompatibility and can be used as a nanomedicine for the clinical treatment of NCSCL [63,64].

In this study, the Rg1-CDs with fluorescence excitation dependence were synthesised using Rg1 as the only precursor. The surface of the Rg1-CDs was rich in hydrophilic functional groups with a diameter of approximately 4.0 ± 1.4 nm. The Rg1-CDs possessed good stability under various conditions, including high salt-ion solution, strong acid, strong base, and UV-lamp irradiation, and thus qualify as potential nanomedicines. In vitro cell experiments showed that the Rg1-CDs significantly inhibited A549 cell growth in a dose-dependent manner. the Rg1-CDs inhibited the clonal viability and migration ability of A549 cells and induced G2/M phase arrest and apoptosis in A549 cells. The Rg1-CDs damaged mitochondria and reduced MMP levels by increasing ROS levels and Ca2+ release, whereas activating the caspase pathway promoted apoptosis in A549 cells. We screened 338 differentially expressed proteins between the model and Rg1-CDs groups. The KEGG database was used to analyse the pathway for these differentially expressed proteins. The results showed that the differential proteins were mainly involved in the MAPK pathway, in which the Rg1-CDs upregulated the expression of p-MKK3, MKK3, p-p38, p38, p-HSP27, and HSP27 and downregulated the expression of MSK1. In vivo experiments in mice showed that the Rg1-CDs could significantly inhibit the volume and weight of mouse NSCLC tumours, and promote the apoptosis of NSCLC tumour cells. Moreover, the inhibitory effect of the Rg1-CDs on NSCLC tumours in mice was superior to that of the chemotherapy drug CDDP and exhibited few side effects. Changes in the expression levels of caspase and MAPK pathway-related proteins were compared by IHC staining of mouse NSCLC tumour tissues in the model and Rg1-CDs groups. This further demonstrated that the Rg1-CDs inhibited NSCLC tumour growth by regulating both the caspase and MAPK pathways in vitro and in vivo. The ELISA results suggested that the Rg1-CDs reduce inflammation in the blood of mice and inhibit tumour malignancy and metastasis to some extent. Finally, we confirmed that high-dose Rg1-CDs treatment had little effect on the health of mice. The Rg1-CDs based on herbal extracts showed good biocompatibility, low biological toxicity, and high tumour inhibitory effects and can be applied as a potential nanomedicine for the clinical treatment of NCSCL in the future.
For detailed information about all experiments, please refer to the Supporting Information.

All procedures were carried out in accordance with the guidelines for the care and use of animals in China, and were approved by the Animal Experiment Ethics Committee of Changchun University of Chinese Medicine (Grant No. 2023070).
